title: Anthropic finds robot performance depends heavily on the control layer
friendlyTitle: Claude works better when a robot handles the small movements
summary: Anthropic tested language models on simulated and physical robots, from pendulum control to navigation and object manipulation. Models mostly struggled when commanding joints directly, but newer systems performed much better when supervising pretrained controllers or using higher-level orientation tools.
friendlySummary: Anthropic tested AI on several robots. The AI often failed when it controlled every joint, but did better when another system handled movement and the AI gave higher-level directions.
whyItMatters: Useful embodied AI may arrive through careful division of labour rather than one model controlling every motor. Robot builders can keep fast, proven control systems underneath while using language models for planning, adaptation, and human instructions above them.
friendlyWhyItMatters: Safer robots may use AI for planning while trusted software controls the body. Each part can do the job it handles best.
